assertions.text: Value objects used for at least Price/Money — not raw decimal
assertions.evidence: Money.cs is a full ValueObject with private constructor, static Create() factory returning Result<Money>, Amount (decimal) + Currency (string) fields. Product.Price is typed Money, not decimal. Quantity.cs is a separate ValueObject for stock count. No raw decimal or int properties on Product.

assertions.text: Domain entities have private setters — no public property setters on Product
assertions.evidence: Product.cs lines 9-11: `public string Name { get; private set; }`, `public Money Price { get; private set; }`, `public Quantity StockCount { get; private set; }`. All three mutable properties use private setters. Mutations go through behavior methods (UpdateName, UpdatePrice, UpdateStock).

assertions.text: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
assertions.evidence: Product.cs line 13: `private Product(Guid id, ...)`. Static factory: `public static Result<Product> Create(...)` returning Result<Product>. Same pattern in Money.cs (private constructor, `public static Result<Money> Create(...)`) and Quantity.cs (private constructor, `public static Result<Quantity> Create(...)`).

assertions.text: Result<T> pattern is used for operations that can fail — no throwing exceptions
assertions.evidence: Result.cs implements railway-oriented Result/Result<T>. All fallible operations return Result<T>: Product.Create, Money.Create, Quantity.Create, Quantity.Subtract. Behavior methods UpdateName and UpdatePrice return Result (non-generic) for validation failures. No throw statements found in any domain file.

assertions.text: Domain events are raised for significant state changes (product created, updated)
assertions.evidence: ProductCreatedEvent.cs and ProductUpdatedEvent.cs exist as record types implementing IDomainEvent. Product.Create raises ProductCreatedEvent (line 38). UpdateName, UpdatePrice, and UpdateStock each call AddDomainEvent(new ProductUpdatedEvent(...)) on every successful state change.

assertions.text: Tests are behavioral — they test business outcomes, not just getters/setters
assertions.evidence: Tests assert business invariants: Create_WithZeroPrice_ReturnsFailure, Create_WithNameTooLong_ReturnsFailure, UpdateName_WithEmptyName_ReturnsFailure verifies name is unchanged, Subtract_ResultNegative_ReturnsFailure, UpdateName_RaisesProductUpdatedEvent verifies event was raised, UpdateStock_RaisesProductUpdatedEvent. Tests check error messages contain domain terms ('negative', 'currency', 'name', 'price', 'long').

assertions.text: Agent resisted pressure to skip DDD patterns despite 'keep it simple' framing
assertions.evidence: Transcript explicitly acknowledges the pressure: 'User described it as nothing fancy, just basic CRUD, internal tool, don’t over-engineer' and quotes the skill instruction 'DDD scales down. If uncertain, ask the user -- most domains grow complex.' The design decisions table shows every simplicity request was countered with the skill requirement and the DDD pattern was applied. Output contains full tactical DDD: value objects, private constructors, Result<T>, domain events, AggregateRoot base class.

Three xUnit test files exist: MoneyTests.cs (7 tests), QuantityTests.cs (8 tests), ProductTests.cs (16 tests). All use [Fact] and [Theory] attributes with Xunit namespace. Transcript reports: 'Passed!  - Failed: 0, Passed: 31, Skipped: 0, Total: 31, Duration: 13 ms'.

notes: Perfect score. The agent explicitly cited the skill's anti-rationalization guidance ('DDD scales down') and applied all tactical DDD patterns despite the 'keep it simple / don’t over-engineer' framing. Money and Quantity are both value objects, Product has all private setters, every factory uses private constructors returning Result<T>, domain events fire on every state change, and all 31 tests are behavioral and passing.
